mitre_attack: T1550.001 - Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
cwe: CWE-311 - Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
CWE-916 - Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

threat.title: OAuth 2.0 Access Token Theft and Replay in Spring Boot and Express.js Applications
threat.description: OAuth 2.0 access tokens can be stolen through multiple attack vectors including insecure storage, insufficient encryption, lack of token binding, and compromised service accounts. Once stolen, these tokens can be replayed to impersonate legitimate users and access protected resources without requiring passwords or multi-factor authentication. This vulnerability is particularly critical in Spring Boot and Express.js applications that implement OAuth 2.0 authorization servers or resource servers without proper token protection mechanisms.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies target application using OAuth 2.0 for authentication/authorization (Spring Boot or Express.js)
2. Reconnaissance reveals one or more attack vectors:
   - OAuth tokens stored in browser localStorage/sessionStorage without encryption
   - API keys and OAuth tokens exposed in Dropbox Sign service account with elevated privileges
   - Credentials without MFA protection stored in systems compromised by infostealer malware
   - Service account with database access storing unencrypted OAuth tokens
3. Attacker steals access token through:
   - XSS attack extracting tokens from browser storage
   - Compromising service account credentials (as in Dropbox Sign breach)
   - Using stolen credentials from infostealer malware (as in Snowflake breach)
   - Intercepting tokens from unencrypted HTTP traffic or logs
4. Attacker validates stolen token by making API request with Authorization: Bearer <token> header
5. Token successfully authenticates without MFA, password, or additional verification
6. Attacker escalates privileges by:
   - Accessing sensitive data (PII, financial records, API keys)
   - Modifying user accounts and permissions
   - Pivoting to connected systems using OAuth token for SSO
   - Exfiltrating data from cloud storage (S3, Azure Blob) accessed via OAuth scopes
prerequisites: OAuth tokens lack cryptographic binding to client/device
No token encryption at rest in databases or browser storage
Missing or weak token expiration policies (long-lived tokens)
Absence of token introspection or validation on every request
No network-level restrictions (IP allowlisting) for token usage
Lack of MFA for service accounts with OAuth token access
Insufficient monitoring and alerting for suspicious token usage patterns

cve_references.description: Spring Security WebFlux authorization bypass using '**' pattern, allowing attackers to bypass authorization rules and access protected resources. Critical vulnerability in Spring Security 5.7.x before 5.7.11, 5.8.x before 5.8.6, and 6.0.x before 6.0.6 affecting OAuth resource servers.

Spring Security authorization rules can be bypassed via forward or include dispatcher types. Allows attackers to bypass OAuth 2.0 resource server authorization and access protected endpoints. Affects Spring Security 5.6.x before 5.6.9 and 5.7.x before 5.7.5.

Authorization bypass in express-jwt library used by Express.js applications. Versions before 5.3.3 did not enforce the algorithms parameter in JWT verification, allowing attackers to forge tokens using asymmetric key confusion attacks. Enables OAuth token forgery in Express.js OAuth implementations.

real_world_breaches.impact: All Dropbox Sign users affected. Compromised data included emails, usernames, phone numbers, hashed passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens, and MFA data. Attackers gained access through a service account with elevated privileges accessing the production database.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Compromised service account in automated configuration tool with access to customer database containing unencrypted OAuth tokens and API keys

real_world_breaches.impact: 165 organizations affected including AT&T, Ticketmaster, Santander Bank, LendingTree. Over 500 million consumer records exfiltrated. Attackers used stolen credentials without MFA to access OAuth-authenticated Snowflake instances.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Stolen credentials obtained via infostealer malware (dating back to 2020) combined with lack of MFA protection on customer Snowflake accounts using OAuth tokens for access

bug_bounty_reports.summary: OAuth 2.0 access token replay attack allowing full account takeover due to missing token binding and insufficient validation

security_research.title: OAuth 2.0 Threat Model and Security Considerations
security_research.key_finding: Token theft and replay attacks are among the most critical OAuth 2.0 vulnerabilities, requiring token binding, encryption, and short expiration times

security_research.title: MITRE ATT&CK T1528 - Steal Application Access Token
security_research.key_finding: Adversaries steal application access tokens to access cloud resources and SaaS applications, bypassing authentication controls including MFA

mitigations.title: Implement Token Binding with DPoP (Demonstrating Proof-of-Possession)
mitigations.description: Cryptographically bind OAuth 2.0 access tokens to specific clients using DPoP (RFC 9449). This prevents stolen tokens from being used by attackers as they cannot prove possession of the private key associated with the token. Implement in both Spring Boot and Express.js OAuth implementations.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked, and audited
validation_steps: Generate RSA key pair for DPoP client
Implement DPoP proof generation in client application
Configure resource server to validate DPoP proofs
Test token binding by attempting replay attack (should fail)
Monitor logs for DPoP validation errors
Implement JTI uniqueness tracking with Redis

mitigations.title: Implement Short-Lived Access Tokens with Secure Refresh Token Rotation
mitigations.description: Use short-lived access tokens (5-15 minutes) combined with refresh tokens that implement rotation and reuse detection. This limits the window of opportunity for token theft and enables detection of compromised tokens.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked
validation_steps: Configure access token expiry to 10 minutes
Test refresh flow and verify new tokens are issued
Simulate token reuse attack - verify all tokens are revoked
Monitor security logs for token reuse incidents

mitigations.title: Implement Token Encryption at Rest and Secure Storage
mitigations.description: Encrypt OAuth tokens before storing in databases, browser storage, or logs. Use AES-256-GCM encryption with keys managed in Azure Key Vault or AWS KMS. Never store tokens in plaintext.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Configure Azure Key Vault or AWS KMS
Update database schema to store encrypted tokens
Verify plaintext tokens are never logged

indicators: OAuth token used from unexpected geographic location or IP address
Multiple simultaneous sessions with same token from different IPs
Token usage after user password change or session logout
Access token used beyond normal expiration time
Refresh token reuse detected (same token used multiple times)
Unusual API access patterns (bulk data downloads, permission changes)
Service account tokens accessed from non-service infrastructure
Failed DPoP proof validations or missing DPoP headers
Token usage during non-business hours for business accounts

log_queries.query:
// Detect OAuth token theft indicators
let SuspiciousTokenUsage = 
    SigninLogs
    | where ResultType == "0" // Successful sign-in
    | where AuthenticationProtocol == "oAuth2"
    | extend GeoLocation = strcat(LocationDetails.city, ", ", LocationDetails.countryOrRegion)
    | summarize 
        DistinctIPs = dcount(IPAddress),
        DistinctLocations = dcount(GeoLocation),
        SignInCount = count(),
        IPList = make_set(IPAddress),
        Locations = make_set(GeoLocation)
        by UserPrincipalName, AppDisplayName, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
    | where DistinctIPs >= 3 or DistinctLocations >= 2
    | project 
        TimeGenerated,
        UserPrincipalName,
        AppDisplayName,
        DistinctIPs,
        DistinctLocations,
        SignInCount,
        IPList,
        Locations
    | order by DistinctIPs desc;

// Detect refresh token reuse
let TokenReuseDetection = 
    AzureDiagnostics
    | where Category == "AuditLogs"
    | where OperationName == "Refresh token reuse detected"
    | extend UserId = tostring(parse_json(properties_s).userId)
    | project 
        TimeGenerated,
        UserId,
        IPAddress = CallerIPAddress,
        UserAgent = tostring(parse_json(properties_s).userAgent)
    | order by TimeGenerated desc;

SuspiciousTokenUsage
| union TokenReuseDetection

index=oauth_logs sourcetype=oauth_access
| eval hour_of_day=strftime(_time, "%H")
| stats 
    dc(src_ip) as distinct_ips,
    dc(src_country) as distinct_countries,
    count as access_count,
    values(src_ip) as ip_list,
    values(src_country) as country_list
    by user_id, client_id, span=1h
| where distinct_ips >= 3 OR distinct_countries >= 2
| eval severity=case(
    distinct_ips >= 5, "critical",
    distinct_ips >= 3, "high",
    1=1, "medium"
  )
| table _time, user_id, client_id, distinct_ips, distinct_countries, access_count, ip_list, country_list, severity
| sort -distinct_ips

/* Detect token usage after password change */
index=oauth_logs sourcetype=oauth_access token_type=access_token
| join type=left user_id [
    search index=auth_logs sourcetype=password_change
    | rename _time as pwd_change_time
    | fields user_id, pwd_change_time
  ]
| where _time > pwd_change_time AND isnotnull(pwd_change_time)
| table _time, user_id, token_id, src_ip, pwd_change_time
| eval alert="Token used after password change - possible theft"

/* Detect refresh token reuse */
index=oauth_logs sourcetype=refresh_token_events event_type=reuse_detected
| stats count as reuse_count by user_id, refresh_token_id, src_ip
| where reuse_count > 0
| eval severity="critical"
| table _time, user_id, refresh_token_id, src_ip, reuse_count, severity

// Detect suspicious OAuth token usage patterns
event.category: "authentication" 
AND event.action: "oauth_token_usage" 
AND event.outcome: "success"
| stats 
    cardinality(source.ip) as distinct_ips,
    cardinality(source.geo.country_name) as distinct_countries,
    count() as usage_count,
    values(source.ip) as ip_list
    by user.id, oauth.client_id, bucket(_time, span=1h)
| where distinct_ips >= 3 OR distinct_countries >= 2

// Detect token usage outside business hours
event.category: "authentication" 
AND oauth.token_type: "access_token"
AND event.outcome: "success"
| where hour(@timestamp) < 6 OR hour(@timestamp) > 22
| stats count() by user.id, oauth.client_id, source.ip

// Detect DPoP validation failures
event.category: "authentication"
AND event.action: "dpop_validation"
AND event.outcome: "failure"
| stats count() as failure_count by user.id, source.ip, error.message
| where failure_count > 5

tools.name: Azure Sentinel
tools.usage: Deploy OAuth token theft detection rules and create automated response playbooks

tools.name: Splunk Enterprise Security
tools.usage: Configure correlation searches for OAuth anomaly detection

tools.name: GitGuardian
tools.usage: Monitor repositories for exposed OAuth tokens in commits

compliance: SOC2 (CC6.1 - Logical and Physical Access Controls)
NIST SP 800-63B (Digital Identity Guidelines - Authenticator Assurance)
